The perception that you are a sharp person would essentially, let's look at this here, let's look at the opposite of sharpness, would be you're a dullard, you're dull, you're not sharp as a tack, right? So I guess my question to you would be, let's say you met someone and in about three or four seconds you had them sized up as being a dullard. How intent would you be on continuing the conversation and listening to them? Not very. Not very intent, right? So obviously this idea that if you come up as being dull and not that bright, people are not going to want to be around you and not going to listen to you. The idea to come off sharp, essentially to sound good, to sound smart, to sound sophisticated, that essentially makes people want to listen. We had this old commercial many years ago in the US, it was called EF Hutton. When EF Hutton speaks, everyone listens and they'd be a loud, a craddy room and some guy would say, well I'm from EF Hutton and almost the whole room would grow silent. On some level that's really what sharpness is about. So when you open up your mouth, they have three or four seconds, they're like, I want to listen to you. It's perceived, it's not logical, it's a perception that you create and you create that through tonality, that's snappy, that's punchy, where you enunciate your words correctly, you're moving your body the right way. So it's like, I say, hi, hi Victoria, this is Jordan here. How are you doing, Victoria? And you're like, what a duller this guy is. Or I say, hey Victoria, Jordan, nice to meet you. And you're like, whoa, okay. And you immediately key into me, right? So that's really what being sharp is all about. I always say there's three things that you have to have in those first four seconds of a conversation. And if you don't have them, good luck trying to influence people. I'll tell you what they are. Number one, sharpest attack. You got to come off in those first few seconds, sharpest attack. Number two, enthusiastic as hell. You got to be enthusiastic about what you're talking about. Just to come through, either the phone or in person, ooze out of your pores. And enthusiasm is like, oh my God, it's the greatest. You don't have to yell and scream at someone. Enthusiasm could be an always spur. It's that power source that you have that just like it comes from within you. It's that bottled energy that you can unleash at any moment. It's below your voice box. It's not yelling and screaming. It's very, very different. But that enthusiasm, sharpest attack, enthusiastic as hell. And number three, which happens to be the other competency you mentioned, it being an expert in your field to be perceived as an authority figure. Now here's the deal. You have always, since where Yee Beg been trained to obey authority, to respect authority, to listen to authority, right? Whether we were told that, you know, the policeman's got the badge, you don't argue with the policeman, you listen to your teacher, as an authority figure, we respect our elders. This is how we've been taught to behave in society. The same holds true even in adults. If they perceive the person and their speech to another adult as the authority figure, they'll tend to comply and listen to them, at least allow themselves to be influenced. So if in those first few seconds, you don't come across as an expert in your field, as a figure of authority, then very, very difficult to influence. It's like, imagine the doctor. If, you know, a doctor comes into the room to see you, he's wearing his white coat, he's got his thing, and he's got a stethoscope on, right? And he's got his chart, and you look at him, he's like, ooh, he's the doctor. You know, implicit with the uniform comes, he's been through 10 years of schooling. He's, you know, dissected bodies. He's saved lives. He knows how to cure the sick and all that sort of stuff, right? So implicit with how the doctor is licensed, his name is, you know, doctor so-and-so, is that he's a figure of authority. Well, the question is, how do you get to that same spot without having the license, without being a doctor, right? How do you become essentially a doctor of what it is you're, you know, trying to influence someone about us, whether it's a product or a good service, an investment, it doesn't matter what it is, right? Well, the way you do that is by coming off as a figure of authority. And again, it comes from your tonality, it comes from your body language, it comes from how you dress if you're in person. Obviously, simply by wearing a suit and tie, you create the perception of authority, okay? And it certainly comes from the right amount of eye contact, and we'll go through all these things in particular. It comes from how you shake hands, how you just carry yourself, and of course, your tonalities of, you know, just simply sounding, sounding, I'm not saying being smart, sounding smart, of saying specific industry terminology that maybe is just a bit above where they are, but it's simple enough, they still get your meaning, but you just sound like you know what you're talking about. And you don't sound like you know what you're saying, you sound. That's to say, you know, I said, you know, you say you sound like you know what you're talking about, and people say, what the hell is he talking about? You say, so you sound like you know what you're talking about. And then people say, whoa, he must be serious. That's impact. That's authority. Simply put, my tonality.
